text.NABRE: Then Judas sent them this peaceful message: “Let us cross your territory in order to reach our own; no one will harm you; we will only march through.” But they would not open to him.
text.NRSV: Judas sent them this friendly message, ‘Let us pass through your land to get to our land. No one will do you harm; we will simply pass by on foot.’ But they refused to open to him.

text.DRB: And Judas commanded proclamation to be made in the camp, that they should approach every man in the place, wherein he was.
text.KJV: Wherefore Iudas commaunded a proclamation to be made throughout the hoste, that euery man should pitch his tent in the place where he was.
text.NABRE: So Judas ordered a proclamation to be made in the camp that everyone should take up positions where they were.
text.NRSV: Then Judas ordered proclamation to be made to the army that all should encamp where they were.

text.DRB: and the men of strength approached, and he assaulted that city all the day, and all the night, and the city was delivered into his hand:
text.KJV: So the souldiers pitched, and assaulted the city all that day, and all that night, till at the length the city was deliuered into his hands:
text.NABRE: When the men of the army took up their positions, he assaulted the city all that day and night, and it was delivered into his hand.
text.NRSV: So the men of the forces encamped, and he fought against the town all that day and all the night, and the town was delivered into his hands.

text.DRB: & they slew every male in the edge of the sword, and he rooted it up, and took the spoils thereof, & passed through all the city upon the slain.
text.KJV: Who then slew all the males with the edge of the sword, and rased the city, and tooke the spoiles therof, and passed through the city ouer them that were slaine.
text.NABRE: He put every male to the sword, leveled the city, took spoils and passed through it over the slain.
text.NRSV: He destroyed every male by the edge of the sword, and razed and plundered the town. Then he passed through the town over the bodies of the dead.

text.DRB: And they passed over Jordan in the great plain, against the face of Bethsan.
text.KJV: After this went they ouer Iordan, into the great plaine before Bethsan.
text.NABRE: Then they crossed the Jordan to the great plain in front of Beth-shan;
text.NRSV: Then they crossed the Jordan into the large plain before Beth-shan.

text.DRB: And Judas gathered together the hindmost, & he exhorted the people throughout all the way, till they came into the land of Juda:
text.KJV: And Iudas gathered together those that came behind, and exhorted the people all the way through, till they came into the land of Iudea.
text.NABRE: and Judas kept gathering the stragglers and encouraging the people the whole way, until he reached the land of Judah.
text.NRSV: Judas kept rallying the laggards and encouraging the people all the way until he came to the land of Judah.

text.DRB: and they went up into mount Sion with joy and gladness, and offered holocausts because none of them was fallen till they returned in peace.
text.KJV: So they went vp to mount Sion with ioy and gladnesse, where they offered burnt offerings, because not one of them were slaine, vntill they had returned in peace.
text.NABRE: They ascended Mount Zion in joy and gladness and sacrificed burnt offerings, because not one of them had fallen; they had returned in safety.
text.NRSV: So they went up to Mount Zion with joy and gladness, and offered burnt-offerings, because they had returned in safety; not one of them had fallen.

text.DRB: And in the days that Judas & Jonathas were in the land of Galaad, & Simon his brother in Galilee against the face of Ptolomais,
text.KJV: Now what time as Iudas and Ionathan were in the land of Galaad, and Simon his brother in Galilee before Ptolemais,
text.NABRE: Joseph and Azariah Defeated. In those days when Judas and Jonathan were in the land of Gilead, and Simon his brother was in Galilee opposite Ptolemais,
text.NRSV: Joseph and Azariah Defeated Now while Judas and Jonathan were in Gilead and their brother Simon was in Galilee before Ptolemais,

text.DRB: Joseph the son of Zacharie heard, & Azarias, the princes of the band, the things well achieved, and the battles that were made,
text.KJV: Ioseph the sonne of Zacharias, and Azarias, captaines of the garisons, heard of the valiant actes and warlike deeds which they had done.
text.NABRE: Joseph, son of Zechariah, and Azariah, the leaders of the army, heard about the brave deeds and the fighting that they were doing.
text.NRSV: Joseph son of Zechariah, and Azariah, the commanders of the forces, heard of their brave deeds and of the heroic war they had fought.

text.DRB: and he said: Let us also make us a name, and let us go fight against the heathen, that are round about us.
text.KJV: Wherefore they said, Let vs also get vs a name, and goe fight against the heathen that are round about vs.
text.NABRE: They said, “Let us also make a name for ourselves by going out and fighting against the Gentiles around us.”
text.NRSV: So they said, ‘Let us also make a name for ourselves; let us go and make war on the Gentiles around us.’

text.DRB: And he commanded them that were in his army, and they went to Jamnia.
text.KJV: So when they had giuen charge vnto the garison that was with them, they went towards Iamnia.
text.NABRE: They gave orders to those of their army who were with them, and marched against Jamnia.
text.NRSV: So they issued orders to the men of the forces that were with them and marched against Jamnia.

text.DRB: And Gorgias went forth out of the city, and his men to meet them into battle.
text.KJV: Then came Gorgias and his men out of the citie to fight against them.
text.NABRE: But Gorgias and his men came out of the city to meet them in battle.
text.NRSV: Gorgias and his men came out of the town to meet them in battle.

text.DRB: And Joseph and Azarias were chased in flight even unto the borders of Jurie: and there fell that day of the people of Israel about two thousand men, and there was made a great tumult in the people:
text.KJV: And so it was, that Ioseph and Azarias were put to flight, and pursued vnto the borders of Iudea, and there were slaine that day of the people of Israel about two thousand men.
text.NABRE: Joseph and Azariah were routed and were pursued to the frontiers of Judea, and about two thousand Israelites fell that day.
text.NRSV: Then Joseph and Azariah were routed, and were pursued to the borders of Judea; as many as two thousand of the people of Israel fell that day.

text.DRB: because they heard not Judas, & his brethren, thinking that they should do manfully.
text.KJV: Thus was there a great ouerthrow among the children of Israel, because they were not obedient vnto Iudas, and his brethren, but thought to doe some valiant act.
text.NABRE: It was a great setback for the people, because they had not obeyed Judas and his brothers, thinking that they would do brave deeds.
text.NRSV: Thus the people suffered a great rout because, thinking to do a brave deed, they did not listen to Judas and his brothers.

text.DRB: But they were not of the seed of those men by whom salvation was made in Israel.
text.KJV: Moreouer these men came not of the seed of those, by whose hand deliuerance was giuen vnto Israel.
text.NABRE: But they were not of the family through whom Israel’s deliverance was given.
text.NRSV: But they did not belong to the family of those men through whom deliverance was given to Israel.

text.DRB: And the men of Juda were magnified exceedingly in the sight of all Israel, and of all nations where their name was heard.
text.KJV: Howbeit the man Iudas and his brethren were greatly renowned in the sight of all Israel, and of all the heathen wheresoeuer their name was heard of,
text.NABRE: Victories at Hebron and Azotus. The valiant Judas and his brothers were greatly honored in all Israel and among all the Gentiles, wherever their name was heard;
text.NRSV: The man Judas and his brothers were greatly honoured in all Israel and among all the Gentiles, wherever their name was heard.

text.DRB: And they came together unto them, with acclamations for their good success.
text.KJV: Insomuch as the people assembled vnto them with ioyfull acclamations.
text.NABRE: and people gathered about them and praised them.
text.NRSV: People gathered to them and praised them.

text.DRB: And Judas went out, and his brethren, and they expugned the children of Esau in the land, that is toward the South, and they struck Chebron, and her daughters: and the walls thereof and the towers he burnt with fire round about.
text.KJV: Afterward went Iudas foorth with his brethren, and fought against the children of Esau in the land toward the South, where he smote Hebron, and the townes thereof, and pulled downe the fortresse of it, and burnt the townes thereof round about.
text.NABRE: Then Judas and his brothers went out and attacked the Edomites in the land toward the south; he took Hebron and its villages, and he destroyed its strongholds and burned the towers around it.
text.NRSV: Success at Hebron and Philistia Then Judas and his brothers went out and fought the descendants of Esau in the land to the south. He struck Hebron and its villages and tore down its strongholds and burned its towers on all sides.

text.DRB: And he removed the camp to go into the land of the aliens, and he walked through Samaria.
text.KJV: From thence he remoued to goe into the land of the Philistines, and passed through Samaria.
text.NABRE: He then set out for the land of the foreigners and passed through Marisa.
text.NRSV: Then he marched off to go into the land of the Philistines, and passed through Marisa.

text.DRB: In that day fell the Priests in battle, whilst they will do manfully, whilst they go forth without counsel into battle.
text.KJV: At that time certaine priests desirous to shew their valour, were slaine in battell, for that they went out to fight vnaduisedly.
text.NABRE: On that day some priests fell in battle who had gone out rashly to fight in their desire to do brave deeds.
text.NRSV: On that day some priests, who wished to do a brave deed, fell in battle, for they went out to battle unwisely.

text.KJV: So Iudas turned to Azotus in the land of the Philistines, and when he had pulled downe their altars, and burnt their carued images with fire, and spoiled their cities, he returned into the land of Iudea.
text.NABRE: Judas then turned toward Azotus in the land of the foreigners. He destroyed their altars and burned the carved images of their gods; and after plundering their cities he returned to the land of Judah.
text.NRSV: But Judas turned aside to Azotus in the land of the Philistines; he tore down their altars, and the carved images of their gods he burned with fire; he plundered the towns and returned to the land of Judah.
text.DRB: And Judas declined to Azotus into the land of the aliens, and he threw down their altars, and the sculptiles of their gods he burnt with fire: and he took the spoils of the cities, and returned into the land of Juda.

text.KJV: About that time king Antiochus trauailing through the high countreys, heard say that Elimais in the countrey of Persia, was a citie greatly renowned for riches, siluer, and gold,
text.NABRE: Chapter 6 As King Antiochus passed through the eastern provinces, he heard that in Persia there was a city, Elam, famous for its wealth in silver and gold,
text.NRSV: The Last Days of Antiochus Epiphanes 6
text.DRB: And king Antiochus walked through the higher countries, and he heard that there was a city Elymais in Persis, most noble, and plentiful in silver & gold,
and a temple therein exceeding rich: and coverings thereof gold, and brigantines, and shields, which Alexander Philip's son the king of Macedonia that reigned first in Greece left there.
text.KJV: And that there was in it a very rich temple, wherein were couerings of gold, and brestplates, and shields which Alexander sonne of Philippe the Macedonian King, who reigned first among the Grecians, had left there.
text.NABRE: and that its temple was very rich, containing gold helmets, breastplates, and weapons left there by the first king of the Greeks, Alexander, son of Philip, king of Macedon.
text.NRSV: Its temple was very rich, containing golden shields, breastplates, and weapons left there by Alexander son of Philip, the Macedonian king who first reigned over the Greeks.

text.DRB: And he came, and sought to take the city, and to spoil it, and he could not, because the word was known to them that were in the city.
text.KJV: Wherefore he came and sought to take the citie, and to spoile it, but he was not able, because they of the citie hauing had warning thereof,
text.NABRE: He went therefore and tried to capture and loot the city. But he could not do so, because his plan became known to the people of the city
text.NRSV: So he came and tried to take the city and plunder it, but he could not because his plan had become known to the citizens

text.DRB: And they rose up into battle, and he fled from thence, and departed with great sorrow, and returned into Babylonia.
text.KJV: Rose vp against him in battell: So he fled and departed thence with great heauinesse, and returned to Babylon.
text.NABRE: who rose up in battle against him. So he fled and in great dismay withdrew from there to return to Babylon.
text.NRSV: and they withstood him in battle. So he fled and in great disappointment left there to return to Babylon.

text.DRB: And there came one that told him in Persis, that the camps that were in the land of Juda, were put to flight:
text.KJV: Moreouer there came one, who brought in tidings into Persia, that the armies which went against the land of Iudea, were put to flight:
text.NABRE: While he was in Persia, a messenger brought him news that the armies that had gone into the land of Judah had been routed;
text.NRSV: Then someone came to him in Persia and reported that the armies that had gone into the land of Judah had been routed;

text.DRB: and that Lysias went with great power with the first, and was put to flight before the face of the Jews, and they grew strong with armour, and force, and many spoils: which they took out of the camps, which they destroyed:
text.KJV: And that Lysias who went forth first with a great power, was driuen away of the Iewes, and that they were made strong by the armour, and power, and store of spoiles, which they had gotten of the armies, whom they had destroyed.
text.NABRE: that Lysias had gone at first with a strong army and been driven back; that the people of Judah had grown strong by reason of the arms, wealth, and abundant spoils taken from the armies they had cut down;
text.NRSV: that Lysias had gone first with a strong force, but had turned and fled before the Jews; that the Jews had grown strong from the arms, supplies, and abundant spoils that they had taken from the armies they had cut down;

text.DRB: and that they threw down the abomination which they had builded upon the altar that was in Jerusalem, and the sanctification they compassed about as before with high walls, yea and Bethsura his city,
text.KJV: Also that they had pulled downe the abomination which hee had set vp vpon the altar in Ierusalem, and that they had compassed about the Sanctuarie with high wals as before, and his citie Bethsura.
text.NABRE: that they had pulled down the abomination which he had built upon the altar in Jerusalem; and that they had surrounded with high walls both the sanctuary, as it had been before, and his city of Beth-zur.
text.NRSV: that they had torn down the abomination that he had erected on the altar in Jerusalem; and that they had surrounded the sanctuary with high walls as before, and also Beth-zur, his town.

text.KJV: Now when the king heard these words, he was astonished, and sore moued, whereupon hee laide him downe vpon his bedde, and fell sicke for griefe, because it had not befallen him, as hee looked for.
text.NABRE: When the king heard this news, he was astonished and very much shaken. Sick with grief because his designs had failed, he took to his bed.
text.NRSV: When the king heard this news, he was astounded and badly shaken. He took to his bed and became sick from disappointment, because things had not turned out for him as he had planned.
text.DRB: And it came to pass as the king heard these words, he was sore afraid, and was moved exceedingly: and he lay down upon his bed, and fell into sickness for sorrow, because it was not chanced to him as he thought.
And he was there for many days: because there was renewed in him great sorrowfulness, and he made account to die.
text.KJV: And there hee continued many dayes: for his griefe was euer more and more, and he made account that he should die.
text.NABRE: There he remained many days, assailed by waves of grief, for he thought he was going to die.
text.NRSV: He lay there for many days, because deep disappointment continually gripped him, and he realized that he was dying.

text.DRB: And he called all his friends, & said to them: sleep is departed from mine eyes, and I am fallen away, and my heart is gone for pensiveness:
text.KJV: Wherefore he called for all his friends, and said vnto them, The sleepe is gone from mine eyes, and my heart faileth for very care.
text.NABRE: So he called in all his Friends and said to them: “Sleep has departed from my eyes, and my heart sinks from anxiety.
text.NRSV: So he called all his Friends and said to them, ‘Sleep has departed from my eyes and I am downhearted with worry.

text.DRB: and I said in my heart: Into how great tribulation am I come, & into what waves of sorrow, wherein now I am: who was pleasant, and beloved in my power!
text.KJV: And I thought with my selfe: Into what tribulation am I come, and how great a flood is it wherein now I am? for I was bountifull, and beloued in my power.
text.NABRE: I said to myself: ‘Into what tribulation have I come, and in what floods of sorrow am I now! Yet I was kindly and beloved in my rule.’
text.NRSV: I said to myself, “To what distress I have come! And into what a great flood I now am plunged! For I was kind and beloved in my power.”

text.DRB: But now I remember the evils, that I have done in Jerusalem, from whence also I took away all the spoils of gold, and of silver, that were in it, and I sent to take away the inhabitants of Jewry, without cause.
text.KJV: But now I remember the euils that I did at Ierusalem, and that I tooke all the vessels of gold and siluer that were therein, and sent to destroy the inhabitants of Iudea without a cause.
text.NABRE: But I now recall the evils I did in Jerusalem, when I carried away all the vessels of silver and gold that were in it, and for no cause gave orders that the inhabitants of Judah be destroyed.
text.NRSV: But now I remember the wrong I did in Jerusalem. I seized all its vessels of silver and gold, and I sent to destroy the inhabitants of Judah without good reason.

text.DRB: I know therefore that for this cause have these evils found me: and lo I perish with great sorrow in a strange country.
text.KJV: I perceiue therefore that for this cause these troubles are come vpon me, and behold I perish through great griefe in a strange land.
text.NABRE: I know that this is why these evils have overtaken me; and now I am dying, in bitter grief, in a foreign land.”
text.NRSV: I know that it is because of this that these misfortunes have come upon me; here I am, perishing of bitter disappointment in a strange land.’

text.DRB: And he called Philip, one of his friends, & he made him chief over all his kingdom.
text.KJV: Then called he for Philip one of his friends whom he made ruler ouer all his realme:
text.NABRE: Then he summoned Philip, one of his Friends, and put him in charge of his whole kingdom.
text.NRSV: Then he called for Philip, one of his Friends, and made him ruler over all his kingdom.

text.DRB: And he gave him the crown, and his robe, and ring, that he should bring Antiochus his son, and should bring him up to reign.
text.KJV: And gaue him the crowne and his robe, and his signet, to the end hee should bring vp his sonne Antiochus, and nourish him vp for the kingdome.
text.NABRE: He gave him his diadem, his robe, and his signet ring, so that he might guide the king’s son Antiochus and bring him up to be king.
text.NRSV: He gave him the crown and his robe and the signet, so that he might guide his son Antiochus and bring him up to be king.

text.KJV: So king Antiochus died there in the hundreth forty and ninth yeere.
text.NABRE: So King Antiochus died there in the one hundred and forty-ninth year.
text.NRSV: Thus King Antiochus died there in the one hundred and forty-ninth year.
text.DRB: And Antiochus the king died there in the year an hundreth forty nine.

text.KJV: Now when Lysias knew that the king was dead, he set vp Antiochus his sonne (whom he had brought vp being yong) to reigne in his stead, and his name he called Eupator.
text.NABRE: When Lysias learned that the king was dead, he set up the king’s son Antiochus, whom he had reared as a child, to be king in his place; and he gave him the title Eupator.
text.NRSV: When Lysias learned that the king was dead, he set up Antiochus the king’s son to reign. Lysias had brought him up from boyhood; he named him Eupator.
text.DRB: And Lysias understood that the king was dead, and he appointed Antiochus his son to reign, who he brought up a young man: and he called his name Eupator.
And they that were in the castle, had inclosed Israel round about the holy places: and they sought them evils always, and the strengthening of the Gentiles.
text.KJV: About this time they that were in the towre shut vp the Israelites round about the Sanctuarie, and sought alwayes their hurt, and the strengthening of the heathen.
text.NABRE: Siege of the Citadel. Those in the citadel were hemming Israel in around the sanctuary, continually trying to harm them and to strengthen the Gentiles.
text.NRSV: Renewed Attacks from Syria Meanwhile the garrison in the citadel kept hemming Israel in around the sanctuary. They were trying in every way to harm them and strengthen the Gentiles.

text.DRB: And Judas thought to destroy them: and he called together all the people, to besiege them.
text.KJV: Wherefore Iudas purposing to destroy them, called all the people together to besiege them.
text.NABRE: And so Judas planned to destroy them, and assembled the people to besiege them.
text.NRSV: Judas therefore resolved to destroy them, and assembled all the people to besiege them.

text.DRB: And they came together, and besieged them in the year an hundredth fifty, and they made balists and engines.
text.KJV: So they came together, and besieged them in the hundred and fiftith yeere, and he made mounts for shot against them, and engines:
text.NABRE: So in the one hundred and fiftieth year they assembled and besieged the citadel, for which purpose he constructed platforms and siege engines.
text.NRSV: They gathered together and besieged the citadel in the one hundred and fiftieth year; and he built siege-towers and other engines of war.

text.DRB: And certain of them that were besieged, went forth: and some impious men of Israel joined themselves unto them,
text.KJV: Howbeit certaine of them that were besieged got forth, vnto whom some vngodly men of Israel ioyned themselues.
text.NABRE: But some of the besieged escaped, and some renegade Israelites joined them.
text.NRSV: But some of the garrison escaped from the siege and some of the ungodly Israelites joined them.

text.DRB: and they went to the king, & said: How long doest thou not judgements, & revengest our brethren?
text.KJV: And they went vnto the king and said, How long will it be ere thou execute iudgement, and auenge our brethren?
text.NABRE: They went to the king and said: “How long will you fail to do justice and to avenge our kindred?
text.NRSV: They went to the king and said, ‘How long will you fail to do justice and to avenge our kindred?

text.DRB: We decreed to serve thy father, and to walk in his precepts, and obey his edicts:
text.KJV: We haue beene willing to serue thy father, and to doe as he would haue vs, and to obey his commandements.
text.NABRE: We agreed to serve your father and to follow his orders and obey his edicts.
text.NRSV: We were happy to serve your father, to live by what he said, and to follow his commands.

text.DRB: and the children of our people for this alienated themselves from us, & whosoever were found of us, they were slain, and our inheritances were spoiled.
text.KJV: For which cause they of our nation besiege the towre, and are alienated from vs: Moreouer as many of vs as they could light on, they slew, and spoiled our inheritance.
text.NABRE: And for this our own people have become our enemies; they have put to death as many of us as they could find and have seized our inheritances.
text.NRSV: For this reason the sons of our people besieged the citadel and became hostile to us; moreover, they have put to death as many of us as they have caught, and they have seized our inheritances.

text.DRB: And not to us only have they extended the hand, but also into all our coasts.
text.KJV: Neither haue they stretched out their hand against vs only, but also against all their borders.
text.NABRE: They have acted aggressively not only against us, but throughout their whole territory.
text.NRSV: It is not against us alone that they have stretched out their hands; they have also attacked all the lands on their borders.

text.DRB: and behold they have approached this day to the castle in Jerusalem to take it, and they have fortified a fortress in Bethsura:
text.KJV: And behold this day are they besieging the towre at Ierusalem to take it: the Sanctuary also, and Bethsura haue they fortified.
text.NABRE: Look! Today they have besieged the citadel in Jerusalem in order to capture it, and they have fortified the sanctuary and Beth-zur.
text.NRSV: And see, today they have encamped against the citadel in Jerusalem to take it; they have fortified both the sanctuary and Beth-zur;

text.DRB: and unless thou prevent them more speedily, they will do greater things than these, and thou shalt not be able to win them.
text.KJV: Wherefore if thou doest not preuent them quickly, they wil doe greater things then these, neither shalt thou be able to rule them.
text.NABRE: Unless you act quickly to prevent them, they will do even worse things than these, and you will not be able to stop them.”
text.NRSV: unless you quickly prevent them, they will do still greater things, and you will not be able to stop them.’

text.DRB: And the king was angry, as he heard these things: & he called together all his friends, & the princes of his army, and them that were over the horsemen.
text.KJV: Now when the king heard this, he was angry, and gathered together all his friends, and the captaines of his armie, and those that had charge of the horse.
text.NABRE: When the king heard this he was enraged, and he called together all his Friends, the officers of his army, and the commanders of the cavalry.
text.NRSV: The king was enraged when he heard this. He assembled all his Friends, the commanders of his forces and those in authority.

text.DRB: Yea and of other realms, and of the islands by the sea there came unto him hired armies.
text.KJV: There came also vnto him from other kingdomes, and from Isles of the Sea bands of hired souldiers.
text.NABRE: Mercenary forces also came to him from other kingdoms and from the islands of the seas.
text.NRSV: Mercenary forces also came to him from other kingdoms and from islands of the seas.

text.KJV: So that the number of his armie was an hundred thousand foote men, and twentie thousand horsemen, and two and thirty Elephants exercised in battell.
text.NABRE: His army numbered a hundred thousand footsoldiers, twenty thousand cavalry, and thirty-two elephants trained for war.
text.NRSV: The number of his forces was one hundred thousand foot-soldiers, twenty thousand horsemen, and thirty-two elephants accustomed to war.
text.DRB: And the number of his army was an hundred thousand footmen, and twenty thousand horsemen, and elephants thirty two, taught to battle.
And they came through Idumea, and approached to Bethsura, and fought many days, and they made engines and came forth, and burnt them with fire, and fought manfully.
text.KJV: These went through Idumea, and pitched against Bethsura which they assaulted many daies, making engines of warre: but they came out, and burnt them with fire, and fought valiantly.
text.NABRE: They passed through Idumea and camped before Beth-zur. For many days they attacked it; they constructed siege engines, but the besieged made a sortie and burned these, and they fought bravely.
text.NRSV: They came through Idumea and encamped against Beth-zur, and for many days they fought and built engines of war; but the Jews sallied out and burned these with fire, and fought courageously.

text.DRB: And Judas departed from the castle, & removed the camp to Bethzacaram against the king's camp.
text.KJV: Upon this Iudas remoued from the towre, and pitched in Bathzacharias, ouer against the kings campe.
text.NABRE: Battle of Beth-zechariah. Then Judas marched away from the citadel and moved his camp to Beth-zechariah, opposite the king’s camp.
text.NRSV: The Battle at Beth-zechariah Then Judas marched away from the citadel and encamped at Beth-zechariah, opposite the camp of the king.

text.DRB: And the king arose before it was light, and stirred the bands into fierceness against the way to Bethzacaram: and the armies made themselves ready to the battle, and they sounded with trumpets:
text.KJV: Then the king rising very earely marched fiercely with his host toward Bathzacharias, where his armies made them ready to battell, and sounded the trumpets.
text.NABRE: The king, rising before dawn, moved his force hastily along the road to Beth-zechariah; and the troops prepared for battle and sounded the trumpet.
text.NRSV: Early in the morning the king set out and took his army by a forced march along the road to Beth-zechariah, and his troops made ready for battle and sounded their trumpets.

text.DRB: and to the elephants they shewed the blood of the grape, and of the mulberry, to provoke them to the battle.
text.KJV: And to the end they might prouoke the elephants to fight, they shewed them the blood of grapes & mulberies.
text.NABRE: They made the elephants drunk on the juice of grapes and mulberries to get them ready to fight.
text.NRSV: They offered the elephants the juice of grapes and mulberries, to arouse them for battle.

text.DRB: And they divided the beasts by the legions: & there stood by every elephant a thousand men in coats of mail, and helmets of brass on their heads: and five hundred horsemen set in order were chosen for every beast.
text.KJV: Moreouer, they diuided the beasts among the armies, and for euery elephant they appointed a thousand men, armed with coats of male, and with helmets of brasse on their heads, and besides this, for euery beast were ordained fiue hundred horsemen of the best.
text.NABRE: The beasts were distributed along the phalanxes, each elephant having assigned to it a thousand men in coats of mail, with bronze helmets on their heads, and five hundred picked cavalry.
text.NRSV: They distributed the animals among the phalanxes; with each elephant they stationed a thousand men armed with coats of mail, and with brass helmets on their heads; and five hundred picked horsemen were assigned to each beast.

text.DRB: These before the time wheresoever the beast was, there were they: & whither soever it went, they went, & they departed not from it.
text.KJV: These were ready at euery occasion: wheresoeuer the beast was, and whithersoeuer þ beast went, they went also, neither departed they from him.
text.NABRE: These accompanied the beast wherever it was; wherever it moved, they moved too and never left it.
text.NRSV: These took their position beforehand wherever the animal was; wherever it went, they went with it, and they never left it.

text.DRB: Yea and upon them were strong wooden towers protecting every beast: and upon them engines, and upon every one thirty two men of strength which fought from above: and within the master of the beast.
text.KJV: And vpon the beastes were there strong towres of wood, which couered euery one of them, and were girt fast vnto them with deuices: there were also vpon euery one two and thirtie strong men that fought vpon them, besides the Indian that ruled him.
text.NABRE: Each elephant was outfitted with a strong wooden tower, fastened to it by a harness; each tower held three soldiers who fought from it, besides the Indian driver.
text.NRSV: On the elephants were wooden towers, strong and covered; they were fastened on each animal by special harness, and on each were four armed men who fought from there, and also its Indian driver.

text.DRB: And the residue of the horsemen he placed on this side and on that side into two parts, with trumpets to stir up the army, and to urge them that stood thick together in the legions thereof.
text.KJV: As for the remnant of the horsemen they set them on this side, and that side, at the two parts of the host giuing them signes what to do, and being harnessed all ouer amidst the rankes.
text.NABRE: The remaining cavalry were stationed on one or the other of the two flanks of the army, to harass the enemy and to be protected by the phalanxes.
text.NRSV: The rest of the cavalry were stationed on either side, on the two flanks of the army, to harass the enemy while being themselves protected by the phalanxes.

text.DRB: And as the sun did shine upon the bucklers of gold, and of brass, the mountains glistered therewith, & they glistered as lamps of fire.
text.KJV: Now when the Sunne shone vpon the shields of golde, and brasse, the mountaines glistered therewith, and shined like lampes of fire.
text.NABRE: When the sun shone on the gold and bronze shields, the mountains gleamed with their brightness and blazed like flaming torches.
text.NRSV: When the sun shone on the shields of gold and brass, the hills were ablaze with them and gleamed like flaming torches.

text.DRB: And part of the king's army was severed by the high mountains, & another part by the low places: & they marched warily and orderly.
text.KJV: So part of the kings armie being spred vpon the high mountaines, and part on the valleyes below, they marched on safely, and in order.
text.NABRE: Part of the king’s army spread out along the heights, while some were on low ground, and they marched forward steadily in good order.
text.NRSV: Now a part of the king’s army was spread out on the high hills, and some troops were on the plain, and they advanced steadily and in good order.

text.DRB: And all the inhabitants of the land were moved at the voice of the multitude of them, and the marching of the troop, and the rattling of the armour, for it was an army exceeding great and strong.
text.KJV: Wherefore all that heard the noise of their multitude, and the marching of the company, and the ratling of the harnesse, were moued: for the army was very great and mighty.
text.NABRE: All who heard the noise of their numbers, the tramp of their marching, and the clanging of the arms, trembled; for the army was very great and strong.
text.NRSV: All who heard the noise made by their multitude, by the marching of the multitude and the clanking of their arms, trembled, for the army was very large and strong.

text.DRB: And Judas approached, and his army into battle: and there fell of the king's army six hundred men.
text.KJV: Then Iudas and his host drew neere, and entred into battell, and there were slaine of the kings army, sixe hundred men.
text.NABRE: Judas with his army advanced to fight, and six hundred men of the king’s army fell.
text.NRSV: But Judas and his army advanced to the battle, and six hundred of the king’s army fell.

text.KJV: ¶ Eleazar also (syrnamed) Sauaran, perceiuing that one of the beasts, armed with royall harnesse, was higher then all the rest, and supposing that the king was vpon him,
text.NABRE: Eleazar, called Avaran, saw one of the beasts covered with royal armor and bigger than any of the others, and so he thought the king was on it.
text.NRSV: Now Eleazar, called Avaran, saw that one of the animals was equipped with royal armour. It was taller than all the others, and he supposed that the king was on it.
text.DRB: And Eleazar the son of Saura saw one of the beasts harnessed with the king's harness: & it was eminent above the other beasts; & it seemed to him that the king was on it:
and he gave himself to deliver his people, and to get himself an everlasting name.
text.KJV: Put himselfe in ieopardie, to the end hee might deliuer his people, and get him a perpetuall name:
text.NABRE: He gave up his life to save his people and win an everlasting name for himself.
text.NRSV: So he gave his life to save his people and to win for himself an everlasting name.

text.DRB: And he ran to it boldly in the midst of the legion, killing on the right hand, & on the left, and they were slain of him on this side and that side.
text.KJV: Wherefore hee ranne vpon him courageously through the midst of the battell, slaying on the right hand, and on the left, so that they were diuided from him on both sides.
text.NABRE: He dashed courageously up to it in the middle of the phalanx, killing men right and left, so that they parted before him.
text.NRSV: He courageously ran into the midst of the phalanx to reach it; he killed men right and left, and they parted before him on both sides.

text.KJV: Which done, he crept vnder the Elephant, and thrust him vnder and slew him: whereupon the Elephant fell downe vpon him, and there he died.
text.NABRE: He ran under the elephant, stabbed it and killed it. The beast fell to the ground on top of him, and he died there.
text.NRSV: He got under the elephant, stabbed it from beneath, and killed it; but it fell to the ground upon him and he died.
text.DRB: And he went under the feet of the elephant, and put himself under him, and slew him: and it fell to the ground upon him, and he died there.
And they seeing the king's power, and the fierceness of his army, turned themselves aside from them.
text.KJV: How be it seeing the strength of the king, and the violence of his forces, turned away from them.
text.NABRE: But when Judas’ troops saw the strength of the royal army and the ardor of its forces, they retreated from them.
text.NRSV: When the Jews saw the royal might and the fierce attack of the forces, they turned away in flight.

text.DRB: But the king's camp went up against them unto Jerusalem: and the king's camp approached to Jurie and mount Sion.
text.KJV: ¶ Then the kings armie went vp to Ierusalem to meet them, and the king pitched his tents against Iudea, and against mount Sion.
text.NABRE: The Siege of Jerusalem. Some of the king’s army went up to Jerusalem to attack them, and the king established camps in Judea and at Mount Zion.
text.NRSV: The Siege of the Temple The soldiers of the king’s army went up to Jerusalem against them, and the king encamped in Judea and at Mount Zion.

text.DRB: And he made peace with them that were in Bethsura: & they came forth out of the city, because there were no victuals for them there being shut up, because it was the sabbaths of the land.
text.KJV: But with them that were in Bethsura hee made peace: for they came out of the citie, because they had no victuals there, to endure the siege, it being a yeere of rest to the land.
text.NABRE: He made peace with the people of Beth-zur, and they evacuated the city, because they had no food there to enable them to withstand a siege, for that was a sabbath year in the land.
text.NRSV: He made peace with the people of Beth-zur, and they evacuated the town because they had no provisions there to withstand a siege, since it was a sabbatical year for the land.

text.DRB: And the king took Bethsura: and he placed there a garrison to keep it.
text.KJV: So the King tooke Bethsura, and set a garison there to keepe it.
text.NABRE: The king took Beth-zur and stationed a garrison there to hold it.
text.NRSV: So the king took Beth-zur and stationed a guard there to hold it.

text.DRB: And he turned the place of the sanctification many days: and he placed there arbalists, and engines, and instruments to cast fire, & pieces to cast stones, and arrows, & scorpions to shoot arrows, and slings.
text.KJV: As for the Sanctuarie hee besieged it many dayes: and set there artillerie with engins, and instruments to cast fire and stones, and pieces to cast darts, and slings.
text.NABRE: For many days he besieged the sanctuary, setting up platforms and siege engines, fire-throwers, catapults and mechanical bows for shooting arrows and projectiles.
text.NRSV: Then he encamped before the sanctuary for many days. He set up siege-towers, engines of war to throw fire and stones, machines to shoot arrows, and catapults.

text.DRB: And they also made engines against their engines, and they fought many days.
text.KJV: Whereupon they also made engins, against their engins, and helde them battell a long season.
text.NABRE: The defenders countered by setting up siege engines of their own, and kept up the fight a long time.
text.NRSV: The Jews also made engines of war to match theirs, and fought for many days.

text.DRB: But there were no victuals in the city, for that it was the seventh year: and they that had remained in Jury of the Gentiles, had consumed their remains, that had been laid up.
text.KJV: Yet at the last their vessels being without victuals, (for that it was the seuenth yeere, and they in Iudea that were deliuered from the Gentiles, had eaten vp the residue of the store)
text.NABRE: But there were no provisions in the storerooms, because it was the seventh year, and the reserves had been eaten up by those who had been rescued from the Gentiles and brought to Judea.
text.NRSV: But they had no food in storage, because it was the seventh year; those who had found safety in Judea from the Gentiles had consumed the last of the stores.

text.DRB: And there remained in the holies few men, because famine had prevailed over them: and they were dispersed every man into his place.
text.KJV: There were but a few left in the Sanctuary, because the famine did so preuaile against them, that they were faine to disperse themselues, euery man to his owne place.
text.NABRE: Few men remained in the sanctuary because the famine was too much for them; the rest scattered, each to his own home.
text.NRSV: Only a few men were left in the sanctuary; the rest scattered to their own homes, for the famine proved too much for them.

text.DRB: And Lysias heard that Philip, whom king Antiochus when he lived, had appointed to bring up his son to reign,
text.KJV: At that time Lysias heard say, that Philip (whom Antiochus the King whiles hee liued had appointed to bring vp his sonne Antiochus, that he might be king)
text.NABRE: Peace Treaty. Lysias heard that Philip, whom King Antiochus, before his death, had appointed to train his son Antiochus to be king,
text.NRSV: Syria Offers Terms Then Lysias heard that Philip, whom King Antiochus while still living had appointed to bring up his son Antiochus to be king,

text.DRB: was returned from Persis, and Media, and the army that went with him, and that he sought to take upon him the affairs of the kingdom:
text.KJV: Was returned out of Persia, and Media, and the Kings host also that went with him, and that hee sought to take vnto him the ruling of the affaires.
text.NABRE: had returned from Persia and Media with the army that accompanied the king, and that he was seeking to take over the government.
text.NRSV: had returned from Persia and Media with the forces that had gone with the king, and that he was trying to seize control of the government.

text.DRB: and he hastened to go, and to say to the king, & to the princes of the army: We decay daily, and there is little victual for us, and the place that we besiege, is fenced, and it lieth upon us to take order for the kingdom.
text.KJV: Wherefore hee went in all haste, and said to the King, and the captaines of the host, and the company, Wee decay dayly, and our victuals are but small, and the place wee lay siege vnto is strong: and the affaires of the kingdome lie vpon vs.
text.NABRE: So he hastily decided to withdraw. He said to the king, the leaders of the army, and the soldiers: “We are growing weaker every day, our provisions are scanty, the place we are besieging is strong, and it is our duty to take care of the affairs of the kingdom.
text.NRSV: So he quickly gave orders to withdraw, and said to the king, to the commanders of the forces, and to the troops, ‘Daily we grow weaker, our food supply is scant, the place against which we are fighting is strong, and the affairs of the kingdom press urgently on us.

text.DRB: Now therefore let us give the right hands to these men, and make peace with them, and with all their nation.
text.KJV: Now therefore let vs be friends with these men, and make peace with them, and with all their nation.
text.NABRE: Therefore let us now come to terms with these people and make peace with them and all their nation.
text.NRSV: Now then, let us come to terms with these people, and make peace with them and with all their nation.

text.DRB: And let us decree for them, that they walk in their own ordinances as before. For, because of their ordinances which we despised, they have been wrath, and have done all these things.
text.KJV: And couenant with them, that they shall liue after their Lawes, as they did before: for they are therefore displeased, & haue done all these things because wee abolished their Lawes.
text.NABRE: Let us grant them freedom to live according to their own laws as formerly; it was on account of their laws, which we abolished, that they became enraged and did all these things.”
text.NRSV: Let us agree to let them live by their laws as they did before; for it was on account of their laws that we abolished that they became angry and did all these things.’

text.DRB: And the word was liked in the sight of the king, and of the princes: and he sent unto them to make peace: and they received it.
text.KJV: So the King and the Princes were content: wherefore hee sent vnto them to make peace, and they accepted thereof.
text.NABRE: The proposal pleased the king and the leaders; he sent peace terms to the Jews, and they accepted.
text.NRSV: The speech pleased the king and the commanders, and he sent to the Jews an offer of peace, and they accepted it.

text.DRB: And the king and the princes sware to them: & they came out of the fortress.
text.KJV: Also the King and the Princes made an oath vnto them: whereupon they went out of the strong hold.
text.NABRE: So the king and the leaders swore an oath to them, and on these terms the Jews evacuated the fortification.
text.NRSV: So the king and the commanders gave them their oath. On these conditions the Jews evacuated the stronghold.

text.DRB: And the king entered mount Sion, and saw the munition of the place: and he brake quickly the oath which he sware, and commanded to destroy the wall round about.
text.KJV: Then the King entred into mount Sion, but when hee saw the strength of the place, hee brake his oath that hee had made, and gaue commandement to pull downe the wall round about.
text.NABRE: But when the king entered Mount Zion and saw how the place was fortified, he broke the oath he had sworn and gave orders to tear down the encircling wall.
text.NRSV: But when the king entered Mount Zion and saw what a strong fortress the place was, he broke the oath he had sworn and gave orders to tear down the wall all round.

text.DRB: And he departed in haste, and returned to Antioch, and found Philip ruling over the city: and he fought against him and won the city.
text.KJV: Afterward departed hee in all haste, and returned vnto Antiochia, where hee found Philip to bee master of the citie; So he fought against him, and tooke the citie by force.
text.NABRE: Then he departed in haste and returned to Antioch, where he found Philip in control of the city. He fought against him and took the city by force.
text.NRSV: Then he set off in haste and returned to Antioch. He found Philip in control of the city, but he fought against him, and took the city by force.

text.KJV: In the hundreth and one and fiftieth yeere, Demetrius the sonne of Seleucus departed from Rome, and came vp with a fewe men vnto a citie of the Sea coast, and reigned there.
text.NABRE: Chapter 7 Expedition of Bacchides and Alcimus. In the one hundred and fifty-first year, Demetrius, son of Seleucus, set out from Rome, arrived with a few men at a coastal city, and began to rule there.
text.NRSV: Expedition of Bacchides and Alcimus 7
text.DRB: In the year an hundreth fifty first went forth Demetrius the son of Seleucus from the city of Rome, and he ascended with few men into a city by the sea side, & reigned there.
And it came to pass, as he entered into the house of the kingdom of his fathers, the army took Antiochus, and Lysias, to bring them unto him.
text.KJV: And as he entred into the palace of his ancestors, so it was, that his forces had taken Antiochus and Lysias to bring them vnto him.
text.NABRE: As he was entering the royal palace of his ancestors, the soldiers seized Antiochus and Lysias to bring them to him.
text.NRSV: As he was entering the royal palace of his ancestors, the army seized Antiochus and Lysias to bring them to him.

text.DRB: And the king was known to him: & he said: Shew me not their face.
text.KJV: Wherefore when he knew it, hee said; Let me not see their faces.
text.NABRE: When he was informed of this, he said, “Do not show me their faces.”
text.NRSV: But when this act became known to him, he said, ‘Do not let me see their faces!’

text.DRB: And the army slew them. And Demetrius sat upon the seat of his kingdom:
text.KJV: So his hoste slewe them. Now when Demetrius was set vpon the throne of his kingdome,
text.NABRE: So the soldiers killed them, and Demetrius assumed the royal throne.
text.NRSV: So the army killed them, and Demetrius took his seat on the throne of his kingdom.

text.KJV: There came vnto him all the wicked and vngodly men of Israel, hauing Alcimus (who was desirous to be high Priest) for their captaine.
text.NABRE: Then all the lawless men and renegades of Israel came to him. They were led by Alcimus, who desired to be high priest.
text.NRSV: Then there came to him all the renegade and godless men of Israel; they were led by Alcimus, who wanted to be high priest.
text.DRB: and there came to him the wicked and impious men of Israel: and Alcimus the captain of them, who would be made the Priest.
And they accused the people to the king, saying: Judas and his brethren have destroyed all thy friends, & us he hath destroyed out of our land.
text.KJV: And they accused the people to the king, saying; Iudas and his brethren haue slaine all thy friends, and driuen vs out of our owne land.
text.NABRE: They made this accusation to the king against the people: “Judas and his brothers have destroyed all your friends and have driven us out of our land.
text.NRSV: They brought to the king this accusation against the people: ‘Judas and his brothers have destroyed all your Friends, and have driven us out of our land.

text.DRB: Now therefore send a man, whom thou doest credit, that he may go, and see all the destruction, that he hath done to us, and to the king's countries: and let him punish all his friends, & their aiders.
text.KJV: Now therefore send some man whom thou trustest, and let him goe and see what hauocke he hath made amongst vs, and in the kings land, and let him punish them with all them that aide them.
text.NABRE: So now, send a man whom you trust to go and see all the destruction Judas has wrought on us and on the king’s territory, and let him punish them and all their supporters.”
text.NRSV: Now then, send a man whom you trust; let him go and see all the ruin that Judas has brought on us and on the land of the king, and let him punish them and all who help them.’

text.DRB: And the king chose of his friends Bacchides, that ruled beyond the great river in the kingdom, and was faithful to the king: & he sent him,
text.KJV: Then the king chose Bacchides a friend of the king, who ruled beyond the flood, and was a great man in the kingdome, and faithfull to the king.
text.NABRE: So the king chose Bacchides, one of the King’s Friends, who ruled the province of West-of-Euphrates, a great man in the kingdom, and faithful to the king.
text.NRSV: So the king chose Bacchides, one of the king’s Friends, governor of the province Beyond the River; he was a great man in the kingdom and was faithful to the king.

text.DRB: to see the destruction that Judas hath made: and Alcimus the impious man he appointed to the Priesthood, and gave him in commandment to make revenge upon the children of Israel.
text.KJV: And him hee sent with that wicked Alcimus, whom hee made high Priest, and commanded that he should take vengeance of the children of Israel.
text.NABRE: He sent him and the renegade Alcimus, to whom he granted the high priesthood, with orders to take revenge on the Israelites.
text.NRSV: He sent him, and with him he sent the ungodly Alcimus, whom he made high priest; and he commanded him to take vengeance on the Israelites.

text.DRB: And they arose, and came with a great army into the land of Juda: and they sent messengers, and spake to Judas, and his brethren with peaceable words in guile.
text.KJV: So they departed, and came with a great power into the land of Iudea, where they sent messengers to Iudas and his brethren with peaceable words deceitfully.
text.NABRE: They set out and, on arriving in the land of Judah with a great army, sent messengers who spoke deceitfully to Judas and his brothers in peaceful terms.
text.NRSV: So they marched away and came with a large force into the land of Judah; and he sent messengers to Judas and his brothers with peaceable but treacherous words.

text.DRB: And they attended not to their words: for they saw that they came with a great army.
text.KJV: But they gaue no heede to their words, for they sawe that they were come with a great power.
text.NABRE: But these paid no attention to their words, seeing that they had come with a great army.
text.NRSV: But they paid no attention to their words, for they saw that they had come with a large force.

text.DRB: And there assembled to Alcimus and Bacchides a congregation of the scribes to require the things that are just:
text.KJV: Then did there assemble vnto Alcimus and Bacchides, a company of Scribes, to require iustice.
text.NABRE: A group of scribes, however, gathered about Alcimus and Bacchides to ask for a just agreement.
text.NRSV: Then a group of scribes appeared in a body before Alcimus and Bacchides to ask for just terms.

text.DRB: and first, the Assideans that were in the children of Israel, and they sought peace of them.
text.KJV: Now the Assideans were the first among the children of Israel, that sought peace of them:
text.NABRE: The Hasideans were the first among the Israelites to seek peace with them,
text.NRSV: The Hasideans were first among the Israelites to seek peace from them,

text.DRB: for they said: A man that is a Priest of the seed of Aaron is come, he will not deceive us.
text.KJV: For, said they, one that is a Priest of the seede of Aaron, is come with this armie, and he will doe vs no wrong.
text.NABRE: for they said, “A priest of the line of Aaron has come with the army, and he will not do us any wrong.”
text.NRSV: for they said, ‘A priest of the line of Aaron has come with the army, and he will not harm us.’

text.DRB: And he spake with them peaceable words: and he sware to them, saying: We will do you no harm, nor your friends.
text.KJV: So he spake vnto them peaceably, and sware vnto them, saying; We will procure the harme neither of you nor your friends.
text.NABRE: He spoke with them peacefully and swore to them, “We will not seek to injure you or your friends.”
text.NRSV: Alcimus spoke peaceable words to them and swore this oath to them, ‘We will not seek to injure you or your friends.’

text.DRB: And they believed him. And he took of them threescore men, & slew them in one day, according to the word that is written:
text.KJV: Whereupon they beleeued him: howbeit hee tooke of them threescore men, and slewe them in one day, according to the words which he wrote:
text.NABRE: So they trusted him. But he arrested sixty of them and killed them in one day, according to the words that he wrote:
text.NRSV: So they trusted him; but he seized sixty of them and killed them in one day, in accordance with the word that was written,

text.DRB: The flesh of thy saints, and the blood of them they have shed round about Jerusalem, and there was none to bury them.
text.KJV: The flesh of thy Saints and their blood haue they shed round about Ierusalem, and there was none to bury them.
text.NABRE: “The flesh of your faithful, and their blood they have spilled all around about Jerusalem, and no one was left to bury them.”
text.NRSV: ‘The flesh of your faithful ones and their blood they poured out all around Jerusalem, and there was no one to bury them.’

text.DRB: And there fell fear, and trembling upon all the people, because they said: There is no truth and judgement in them: for they have transgressed the appointment & the oath which they sware.
text.KJV: Wherefore the feare and dread of them fell vpon all the people, who said, There is neither trueth, nor righteousnesse in them; for they haue broken the couenant and othe that they made.
text.NABRE: Then fear and dread of them came upon all the people, who said: “There is no truth or justice among them; they violated the agreement and the oath that they swore.”
text.NRSV: Then the fear and dread of them fell on all the people, for they said, ‘There is no truth or justice in them, for they have violated the agreement and the oath that they swore.’

text.DRB: And Bacchides removed the camp from Jerusalem, and approached to Bethzecha: and he sent, and took many of them that were fled from him, and certain of the people he killed, & threw them into a great pit.
text.KJV: After this remooued Bacchides from Ierusalem, and pitched his tents in Bezeth, where he sent and tooke many of the men that had forsaken him, and certaine of the people also, and when he had slaine them, into the great pit.
text.NABRE: Bacchides withdrew from Jerusalem and camped in Beth-zaith. He had many of the men who deserted to him arrested and some of the people. He killed them and threw them into a great cistern.
text.NRSV: Then Bacchides withdrew from Jerusalem and encamped in Beth-zaith. And he sent and seized many of the men who had deserted to him, and some of the people, and killed them and threw them into a great pit.

text.DRB: And he committed the country to Alcimus, and left with him aid to help him. And Bacchides went away to the king:
text.KJV: Then committed he the countrey to Alcimus, and left with him a power to aide him: so Bacchides went vnto the king.
text.NABRE: He handed the province over to Alcimus, leaving troops to help him, while he himself returned to the king.
text.NRSV: He placed Alcimus in charge of the country and left with him a force to help him; then Bacchides went back to the king.

text.DRB: & Alcimus did what he could for the principality of his Priesthood:
text.KJV: But Alcimus contended for the high Priesthood.
text.NABRE: Alcimus struggled to maintain his high priesthood,
text.NRSV: Alcimus struggled to maintain his high-priesthood,

text.DRB: and there gathered together unto him all that disturbed their people, & they obtained the land of Juda, & made a great plague in Israel.
text.KJV: And vnto him resorted all such as troubled the people, who after they had gotten the land of Iuda into their power, did much hurt in Israel.
text.NABRE: and all those who were troubling the people gathered about him. They took possession of the land of Judah and caused great distress in Israel.
text.NRSV: and all who were troubling their people joined him. They gained control of the land of Judah and did great damage in Israel.

text.DRB: And Judas saw all the evils that Alcimus did, and they that were with him, to the children of Israel, much more than the Gentiles.
text.KJV: Now when Iudas saw all the mischiefe that Alcimus and his company had done among the Israelites, euen aboue the heathen,
text.NABRE: When Judas saw all the evils that Alcimus and those with him were bringing upon the Israelites, even more than the Gentiles had,
text.NRSV: And Judas saw all the wrongs that Alcimus and those with him had done among the Israelites; it was more than the Gentiles had done.

text.DRB: And he went out into all the coasts of Jurie round about, & did vengeance upon the men that revolted, & they ceased to go forth any more into the country.
text.KJV: He went out into all the coast of Iudea round about, and tooke vengeance of them that had reuolted from him, so that they durst no more goe foorth into the countrey.
text.NABRE: he went about all the borders of Judea and took revenge on the men who had deserted, preventing them from going out into the country.
text.NRSV: So Judas went out into all the surrounding parts of Judea, taking vengeance on those who had deserted and preventing those in the city from going out into the country.

text.DRB: But Alcimus saw that Judas prevailed, & they that were with him: & he knew that he could not stand with them, and he went back to the king, & accused them of many crimes.
text.KJV: On the other side, when Alcimus saw that Iudas and his company had gotten the vpper hand, and knew that he was not able to abide their force, he went againe to the king, and said all the worst of them that he could.
text.NABRE: But when Alcimus saw that Judas and his followers were gaining strength and realized that he could not resist them, he returned to the king and accused them of grave crimes.
text.NRSV: When Alcimus saw that Judas and those with him had grown strong, and realized that he could not withstand them, he returned to the king and brought malicious charges against them.

text.KJV: Then the king sent Nicanor one of his honourable princes, a man that bare deadly hate vnto Israel, with commandement to destroy the people.
text.NABRE: Defeat of Nicanor. Then the king sent Nicanor, one of his honored officers, who was a bitter enemy of Israel, with orders to destroy the people.
text.NRSV: Nicanor in Judea Then the king sent Nicanor, one of his honoured princes, who hated and detested Israel, and he commanded him to destroy the people.
text.DRB: And the king sent Nicanor, one of his nobler princes: that practised enmities against Israel: and he commanded him to destroy the people.
And Nicanor came into Jerusalem with a great army, and he sent to Judas & to his brethren in peaceable words with guile,
text.KJV: So Nicanor came to Ierusalem with a great force: and sent vnto Iudas and his brethren deceitfully with friendly words, saying,
text.NABRE: Nicanor came to Jerusalem with a large force and deceitfully sent to Judas and his brothers this peaceable message:
text.NRSV: So Nicanor came to Jerusalem with a large force, and treacherously sent to Judas and his brothers this peaceable message,

text.DRB: saying: Let there be no fighting between me and you: I will come with few men, to see your faces with peace.
text.KJV: Let there be no battell betweene me and you, I will come with a fewe men, that I may see you in peace.
text.NABRE: “Let there be no fight between me and you. I will come with a few men to meet you face to face in peace.”
text.NRSV: ‘Let there be no fighting between you and me; I shall come with a few men to see you face to face in peace.’

text.DRB: And he came to Judas, & they saluted one another peaceably: & the enemies were ready to catch Judas.
text.KJV: He came therefore to Iudas, and they saluted one another peaceably. Howbeit the enemies were prepared to take away Iudas by violence.
text.NABRE: So he came to Judas, and they greeted one another peaceably. But Judas’ enemies were prepared to seize him.
text.NRSV: So he came to Judas, and they greeted one another peaceably; but the enemy were preparing to kidnap Judas.

text.DRB: And the thing was known to Judas that he was come with guile: and he was sore afraid of him, and would not see his face any more.
text.KJV: Which thing after it was knowen to Iudas (to wit) that he came vnto him with deceit, he was sore afraid of him, and would see his face no more.
text.NABRE: When he became aware that Nicanor had come to him with deceit in mind, Judas was afraid of him and would not meet him again.
text.NRSV: It became known to Judas that Nicanor had come to him with treacherous intent, and he was afraid of him and would not meet him again.

text.DRB: And Nicanor knew that his counsel was disclosed: and he went forth to Judas into battle beside Capharsalama.
text.KJV: Nicanor also when he saw that his counsell was discouered, went out to fight against Iudas besides Capharsalama.
text.NABRE: When Nicanor saw that his plan had been discovered, he went out to fight Judas near Capharsalama.
text.NRSV: When Nicanor learned that his plan had been disclosed, he went out to meet Judas in battle near Caphar-salama.

text.DRB: And there fell of Nicanor's army almost five thousand men, & they fled into the city of David.
text.KJV: Where there were slaine of Nicanors side, about fiue thousand men, and fled into the citie of Dauid.
text.NABRE: About five hundred men of Nicanor’s army fell; the rest fled to the City of David.
text.NRSV: About five hundred of the army of Nicanor fell, and the rest fled into the city of David.

text.DRB: And after these words Nicanor went up into mount Sion: and there went forth of the Priests of the people to salute him in peace, and to shew him the holocausts, that were offered for the king.
text.KJV: After this went Nicanor vp to mount Sion, and there came out of the Sanctuarie certaine of the priestes, and certaine of the elders of the people to salute him peaceably, and to shewe him the burnt sacrifice that was offred for the king.
text.NABRE: After this, Nicanor went up to Mount Zion. Some of the priests from the sanctuary and some of the elders of the people came out to greet him peaceably and to show him the burnt offering that was being sacrificed for the king.
text.NRSV: Nicanor Threatens the Temple After these events Nicanor went up to Mount Zion. Some of the priests from the sanctuary and some of the elders of the people came out to greet him peaceably and to show him the burnt-offering that was being offered for the king.

text.DRB: And scorning he contemned them, & polluted them: and he spake proudly,
text.KJV: But he mocked them, and laughed at them, and abused them shamefully, and spake proudly,
text.NABRE: But he mocked and ridiculed them, defiled them, and spoke arrogantly.
text.NRSV: But he mocked them and derided them and defiled them and spoke arrogantly,

text.DRB: and sware with anger, saying: Unless Judas be delivered, & his army into my hands, incontinent when I shall return in peace I will burn this house. And he went out with great anger:
text.KJV: And swore in his wrath, saying, vnlesse Iudas and his hoste be now deliuered into my hands, if euer I come againe in safetie, I will burne vp this house: and with that he went out in a great rage.
text.NABRE: In a rage he swore: “If Judas and his army are not delivered to me at once, when I return victorious I will burn this temple down.” He went away in great anger.
text.NRSV: and in anger he swore this oath, ‘Unless Judas and his army are delivered into my hands this time, then if I return safely I will burn up this house.’ And he went out in great anger.

text.DRB: and the Priests entered in, and stood before the face of the altar and the temple: and weeping they said:
text.KJV: Then the priests entred in, and stood before the altar, and the Temple, weeping, and saying,
text.NABRE: The priests, however, went in and stood before the altar and the sanctuary. They wept and said:
text.NRSV: At this the priests went in and stood before the altar and the temple; they wept and said,

text.DRB: Thou o Lord hast chosen this house for thy name to be invocated therein, that it might be a house of prayer, and obsecration for thy people.
text.KJV: Thou O Lord didst choose this house, to be called by thy Name, and to be a house of prayer and petition for thy people.
text.NABRE: “You have chosen this house to bear your name, to be a house of prayer and supplication for your people.
text.NRSV: ‘You chose this house to be called by your name, and to be for your people a house of prayer and supplication.

text.DRB: Take vengeance upon this man, and his army, and let them fall by the sword: remember their blasphemies, and grant not unto them to be permanent.
text.KJV: Be auenged of this man and his hoste, and let them fall by the sword: Remember their blasphemies, and suffer them not to continue any longer.
text.NABRE: Take revenge on this man and his army, and let them fall by the sword. Remember their blasphemies, and do not let them continue.”
text.NRSV: Take vengeance on this man and on his army, and let them fall by the sword; remember their blasphemies, and let them live no longer.’

text.DRB: And Nicanor went out from Jerusalem, & moved the camp, near to Bethhoron: and the army of Syria met him.
text.KJV: So Nicanor went out of Ierusalem, & pitched his tents in Bethoron, where an hoste out of Syria met him.
text.NABRE: Nicanor left Jerusalem and camped at Beth-horon, where the Syrian army joined him.
text.NRSV: The Death of Nicanor Now Nicanor went out from Jerusalem and encamped in Beth-horon, and the Syrian army joined him.

text.DRB: And Judas approached in Adarsa with three thousand men: and Judas prayed, and said:
text.KJV: But Iudas pitched in Adasa with three thousand men, and there he prayed, saying,
text.NABRE: But Judas camped in Adasa with three thousand men. Here Judas uttered this prayer:
text.NRSV: Judas encamped in Adasa with three thousand men. Then Judas prayed and said,

text.DRB: They that were sent by king Sennacherib, o Lord, because they blasphemed thee, an angel came forth, and struck of them an hundred eighty five thousand:
text.KJV: O Lord, when they that were sent from the king of the Assyrians blasphemed, thine Angel went out, and smote a hundred, fourescore, and fiue thousand of them.
text.NABRE: “When they who were sent by the king blasphemed, your angel went out and killed a hundred and eighty-five thousand of them.
text.NRSV: ‘When the messengers from the king spoke blasphemy, your angel went out and struck down one hundred and eighty-five thousand of the Assyrians.

text.DRB: so destroy this army in our sight today, and let the rest know that he hath spoken it upon the holy places: and judge thou him according to his malice.
text.KJV: Euen so destroy thou this host before vs this day, that the rest may know that he hath spoken blasphemously against thy Sanctuary, and iudge thou him according to his wickednesse.
text.NABRE: In the same way, crush this army before us today, and let the rest know that Nicanor spoke wickedly against your sanctuary; judge him according to his wickedness.”
text.NRSV: So also crush this army before us today; let the rest learn that Nicanor has spoken wickedly against the sanctuary, and judge him according to this wickedness.’

text.KJV: So the thirteenth day of the moneth Adar, the hostes ioyned battell, but Nicanors host was discomfited, & he himselfe was first slaine in the battell.
text.NABRE: The armies met in battle on the thirteenth day of the month Adar. Nicanor’s army was crushed, and he himself was the first to fall in the battle.
text.NRSV: So the armies met in battle on the thirteenth day of the month of Adar. The army of Nicanor was crushed, and he himself was the first to fall in the battle.
text.DRB: And the armies joined battle the thirteenth day of the month Adar: and the camp of Nicanor was discomfited, & himself was slain first in the battle.
And as his army saw that Nicanor was slain, they threw away their weapons, & fled:
text.KJV: Now when Nicanors host saw that he was slaine, they cast away their weapons, and fled.
text.NABRE: When his army saw that Nicanor had fallen, they threw down their weapons and fled.
text.NRSV: When his army saw that Nicanor had fallen, they threw down their arms and fled.

text.DRB: and they pursued them one day's journey from Adazer, even till ye come into Gazara, & with trumpets they sounded after them with significations.
text.KJV: Then they pursued after them a dayes iourney from Adasa, vnto Gasera, sounding an alarme after them with their trumpets.
text.NABRE: The Jews pursued them a day’s journey from Adasa to near Gazara, blowing the trumpets behind them as signals.
text.NRSV: The Jews pursued them a day’s journey, from Adasa as far as Gazara, and as they followed they kept sounding the battle-call on the trumpets.

text.DRB: And they went forth out of all the towns of Jurie round about, and they pushed them with the horns, and they turned again to them, and they were all slain with the sword, and there was not left of them so much as one.
text.KJV: Whereupon they came forth out of all the townes of Iudea round about, and closed them in, so that they turning backe vpon them that pursued them, were all slaine with the sword, and not one of them was left.
text.NABRE: From all the surrounding villages of Judea people came out and outflanked them. They turned them back, and all the enemies fell by the sword; not a single one escaped.
text.NRSV: People came out of all the surrounding villages of Judea, and they outflanked the enemy and drove them back to their pursuers, so that they all fell by the sword; not even one of them was left.

text.KJV: Afterwards they tooke þ spoiles, and the pray, and smote off Nicanors head, & his right hand, which he stretched out so proudly, and brought them away; and hanged them vp, towards Ierusalem.
text.NABRE: Then the Jews collected the spoils and the plunder; they cut off Nicanor’s head and his right arm, which he had lifted up so arrogantly. These they brought and displayed in the sight of Jerusalem.
text.NRSV: Then the Jews seized the spoils and the plunder; they cut off Nicanor’s head and the right hand that he had so arrogantly stretched out, and brought them and displayed them just outside Jerusalem.
text.DRB: And they took the spoils of them for a prey: and Nicanor's head they cut off, and his right hand, which he had proudly stretched forth, & they brought it, & hung it up against Jerusalem.
And the people rejoiced exceedingly, & they spent that day in great gladness.
text.KJV: For this cause the people reioyced greatly, and they kept that day, a day of great gladnesse.
text.NABRE: The people rejoiced greatly, and observed that day as a day of much joy.
text.NRSV: The people rejoiced greatly and celebrated that day as a day of great gladness.

text.DRB: And he ordained that this day should be kept every year the thirteenth of the month Adar.
text.KJV: Moreouer they ordeined to keepe yeerely this day, being the thirteenth of Adar.
text.NABRE: They decreed that it should be observed every year on the thirteenth of Adar.
text.NRSV: They decreed that this day should be celebrated each year on the thirteenth day of Adar.

text.DRB: And the land of Juda was quiet for a few days.
text.KJV: Thus the land of Iuda was in rest a litle while.
text.NABRE: And so for a few days the land of Judah was at rest.
text.NRSV: So the land of Judah had rest for a few days.

text.KJV: Now Iudas had heard of the fame of the Romanes, that they were mighty and valiant men, and such as would louingly accept all that ioyned themselues vnto them, and make a league of amitie with all that came vnto them,
text.NABRE: Chapter 8 Eulogy of the Romans. Judas had heard of the reputation of the Romans. They were valiant fighters and acted amiably to all who took their side. They established a friendly alliance with all who applied to them.
text.NRSV: A Eulogy of the Romans 8
text.DRB: And Judas heard of the name of the Romans, that they are mighty of power, and agree unto all things that are requested of them: and whosoever have come unto them, they have made amity with them, and that they are mighty of power.
And they heard of their battles, and goodly acts, which they did in Galatia, that they overcame them, and brought them under tribute:
text.KJV: And that they were men of great valour: It was told him also of their warres and noble acts which they had done amongst the Galatians, and how they had conquered them, and brought them vnder tribute.
text.NABRE: He was also told of their battles and the brave deeds that they performed against the Gauls, conquering them and forcing them to pay tribute;
text.NRSV: and that they were very strong. He had been told of their wars and of the brave deeds that they were doing among the Gauls, how they had defeated them and forced them to pay tribute,

text.DRB: and how great things they did in the country of Spain, & that they brought into their power the metals of silver and gold, that are there, and possessed every place by their counsel and patience:
text.KJV: And what they had done in þ countrey of Spaine, for the winning of the mines of the siluer & gold which is there
text.NABRE: and what they did in Spain to get possession of the silver and gold mines there.
text.NRSV: and what they had done in the land of Spain to get control of the silver and gold mines there,

text.DRB: and destroyed the places that were very far off from them, and the kings that came upon them from the ends of the earth, and struck them with a great plague: and the rest give them tribute every year.
text.KJV: And that by their policie and patience, they had conquered all that place (though it were very farre from them) and the kings also that came against them from the vttermost part of the earth, till they had discomfited them, & giuen them a great ouerthrow, so that the rest did giue them tribute euery yere.
text.NABRE: By planning and persistence they subjugated the whole region, although it was very remote from their own. They also subjugated the kings who had come against them from the far corners of the earth until they crushed them and inflicted on them severe defeat. The rest paid tribute to them every year.
text.NRSV: and how they had gained control of the whole region by their planning and patience, even though the place was far distant from them. They also subdued the kings who came against them from the ends of the earth, until they crushed them and inflicted great disaster on them; the rest paid them tribute every year.

text.DRB: And Philip and Perses the king of the Ceteans, & the rest that had borne weapon against them, they discomfited in battle, and overcame them:
text.KJV: Besides this, how they had discomfited in battell Philip, and Perseus king of the Citims, with others that lift vp themselues against them, and had ouercome them.
text.NABRE: Philip and Perseus, king of the Macedonians, and the others who opposed them in battle they overwhelmed and subjugated.
text.NRSV: They had crushed in battle and conquered Philip, and King Perseus of the Macedonians, and the others who rose up against them.
